result: I’ve been thinking about how the moon is a liar. It hangs there promising silver constancy, yet it’s always ditching its shape, sneaking off to the far side of the planet where no one can see what it’s up to. Meanwhile we down here keep calendars, set alarms, swear tides into motion like dutiful clerks. I like that kind of cosmic betrayal; it makes room for human variance. Some nights I can’t sleep and the moon looks like a puncture in black velvet, letting a little ancient light leak through. Other nights it’s a polished coin flipped by an unseen thumb, betting on whether I’ll remember to call my mother in the morning. I keep a notebook of lunar phases but never write the date; I draw crude craters instead, trying to map every crater I can see, though the only one I recognize is Copernicus—because saying Copernicus out loud feels like sneezing Latin. The moon never says thank you. It just keeps recycling old photons, thriftier than a grandparent saving string. Still, I wave. I can’t help it. Every month it dies and returns like a story children insist on hearing until the binding disintegrates. Somewhere in the middle of all that revolving I remember I’m also just reflected energy, briefly assembled water and wishful grammar. So I forgive the moon for lying; it’s only reflecting us, a borrowed mirror hoping no one notices the cracks.

It hangs there promising silver constancy, yet it’s always ditching its shape, sneaking off to the far side of the planet where no one can see what it’s up to. Meanwhile we down here keep calendars, set alarms, swear tides into motion like dutiful clerks. I like that kind of cosmic betrayal; it makes room for human variance. Some nights I can’t sleep and the moon looks like a puncture in black velvet, letting a little ancient light leak through. Other nights it’s a polished coin flipped by an unseen thumb, betting on whether I’ll remember to call my mother in the morning. I keep a notebook of lunar phases but never write the date; I draw crude craters instead, trying to map every crater I can see, though the only one I recognize is Copernicus—because saying Copernicus out loud feels like sneezing Latin. The moon never says thank you. It just keeps recycling old photons, thriftier than a grandparent saving string. Still, I wave. I can’t help it. Every month it dies and returns like a story children insist on hearing until the binding disintegrates. Somewhere in the middle of all that revolving I remember I’m also just reflected energy, briefly assembled water and wishful grammar. So I forgive the moon for lying; it’s only reflecting us, a borrowed mirror hoping no one notices the cracks.
